---
title: "<TODO: Module title, e.g. 'Cobb-Douglas'>"
---

<!--
========================================================================
  TEMPLATE for consumer-theory modules.

  TO CREATE A NEW MODULE:
    cp consumer-theory/_template-module.qmd consumer-theory/<slug>.qmd
    Then edit. Replace every <TODO: ...> marker. Delete this comment block.

  STRUCTURE (mirrors perfect-substitutes.qmd and cobb-douglas.qmd):
    1. One-paragraph lede: setup + what the module derives + payoff
    2. ## The model       -- Definition callout + problem statement
    3. ## Solving the problem  -- Proposition + Proof sketch (collapsed)
    4. ## Comparative statics  -- Bullets + Warning callout for traps
    5. ## Worked example  -- Numerical pass with verified arithmetic
    6. ## Figure          -- Code chunk producing brand-styled figure
    7. ## Exercises       -- Three of rising difficulty; collapsible solutions
    8. ## References      -- MWG, Varian, JR, paper sources as relevant

  CROSS-REFERENCE CONVENTIONS:
    Equations:   $$ ... $$ {#eq-<slug>}     referenced as @eq-<slug>
    Sections:    ## Heading {#sec-<slug>}   referenced as @sec-<slug>
    Figures:     #| label: fig-<slug>       referenced as @fig-<slug>

  CALLOUT PATTERNS:
    Definition       ::: {.callout-note title="Definition: ..."}
    Proposition      ::: {.callout-important title="Proposition: ..."}
    Proof sketch     ::: {.callout-tip title="Proof sketch" collapse="true"}
    Warning          ::: {.callout-warning title="..."}
    Exercise         ::: {.callout-note title="Exercise N" appearance="simple"}
    Solution         ::: {.callout-tip title="Solution" collapse="true"}

  WHEN TO DEVIATE FROM THE STRUCTURE ABOVE:
    Firm rules (don't change without a strong reason):
      - The eight section ordering. Readers learn to navigate the section
        once and transfer to every module.
      - The cross-reference labelling conventions. Internal links break
        silently when labels drift.
      - The callout types for Definition / Proposition / Proof sketch /
        Warning / Exercise / Solution. The visual language is part of
        the brand.
    Advisory (deviate when the math wants it):
      - Number of exercises. Three is the default; two suffices for a
        short module, four is fine when the material warrants it.
      - Number of figure panels. Match the structure of the result, not
        the structure of other modules.
      - Proof sketch length. Some results need three lines; some need a
        page of algebra.
    Genuinely module-specific (no template guidance):
      - Choice of utility function, parameter values, and numerical
        examples.
      - Specific cross-references between modules. Add them when the
        math invites them, omit them when it would be forced.

  BEFORE COMMITTING:
    1. grep '<TODO:' consumer-theory/<slug>.qmd  -- must return nothing.
    2. quarto preview -- visually verify figure, callouts, references.
    3. Click every collapsible (Proof sketch, each Solution).
    4. Verify every numerical answer with a Python REPL.
    5. Update consumer-theory/index.qmd to link the new module.

  REMEMBER:
    - Use raw strings for LaTeX in plot labels: r"$\alpha$".
    - Brand colours below are duplicated from sibling modules. This is
      acceptable until ~3 modules; then extract into econschool_plots (M7).
========================================================================
-->

<TODO: One paragraph. State the choice problem, name the payoff of the module
(the "headline insight" a student will take away), and set up notation. Mention
how this case relates to neighbouring modules where relevant — e.g., "Unlike
the perfect-substitutes case, ...".>

## The model

::: {.callout-note title="Definition: <TODO>"}
<TODO: One-line characterisation of the preference family, followed by the
utility function in display math.>
$$
u(x_1, x_2) = \ldots
$$
<TODO: One additional structural fact worth stating up-front — e.g., the MRS,
strict quasi-concavity, the location of the optimum (interior vs corner).>
:::

The consumer's problem is
$$
\max_{x_1, x_2 \ge 0} \; u(x_1, x_2) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad p_1 x_1 + p_2 x_2 \le m,
$$ {#eq-problem}
with $p_1, p_2 > 0$ and $m > 0$.

<TODO: One paragraph explaining which solution technique applies and why —
Lagrangian, corner-by-comparison, dual, etc. — and what to expect (unique
interior optimum, corner, correspondence, ...).>

## Solving the problem

<TODO: Set up the solution. State the relevant first-order or comparison
condition.>

::: {.callout-important title="Proposition: <TODO, e.g. Marshallian demand>"}
<TODO: State the result. Always label the demand equation.>
$$
x_1^*(p_1, p_2, m) = \ldots, \qquad x_2^*(p_1, p_2, m) = \ldots
$$ {#eq-demand}
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Proof sketch" collapse="true"}
<TODO: Two to four paragraphs. Show the key algebra. Don't write a full
rigorous proof — name the technique and let the algebra speak.>
:::

<TODO: Any direct corollaries worth stating inline before moving on. Label
them if the figure or exercises will reference them.>

## Comparative statics

Equation @eq-demand makes the comparative statics immediate.

<TODO: Three to five short paragraphs or bullets. Each one isolates one
partial derivative or elasticity. Use log-derivatives when the demand is
multiplicative. Always state the *sign* and the *interpretation*, not just
the formula.>

::: {.callout-warning title="<TODO: name the trap>"}
<TODO: Identify one common misgeneralisation students make about this case.
Pair this Warning with one of the exercises below so the warning isn't just
abstract advice.>
:::

## Worked example

Take <TODO: parameter values, e.g. $\alpha = 0.4$, $p_1 = 2$, $p_2 = 3$,
$m = 60$>.

<TODO: Compute the demand, indirect utility, expenditure shares, or whatever
the headline objects of the module are. Verify all arithmetic in a Python REPL
before committing — wrong numerical examples poison the rest of the module.

If a comparative-statics demonstration is helpful, add a "now raise $p_1$
from X to Y" pass and show which numbers move and which don't. The CD module
does this and it's high-leverage.>

The geometry is in @sec-figure.

## Figure {#sec-figure}

```{python}
#| label: fig-<TODO-slug>
#| fig-cap: "<TODO: one-sentence caption. Name the parameter values. State what each panel shows. Include LaTeX in the caption where useful — escape backslashes (\\\\alpha) inside the fig-cap string.>"
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: <TODO: 3.5 for one row, 4 for two-panel, 4.5 if dense>
#| echo: false

from dataclasses import dataclass
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Brand palette (Econschool style guide).
# Duplicated across modules; refactor target is econschool_plots (M7).
VIOLET = "#5A1BA6"
TERRACOTTA = "#A03A2C"
SAGE = "#B8C5B5"
CREAM = "#FAF7F2"
INK = "#1F1F1F"


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Params:
    """<TODO: docstring naming the utility function and parameters.>"""
    # TODO: fields for parameters and prices/income


# TODO: plot functions. One per panel if panels are structurally different.
# Signature convention: plot_<panel_name>(ax: plt.Axes, p: Params) -> None.
# Each function configures its own axes (labels, title, spines, aspect).


plt.rcParams.update({
    "text.usetex": False,  # mathtext suffices; usetex is slow in CI
    "font.family": "serif",
    "axes.facecolor": CREAM,
    "figure.facecolor": CREAM,
    "axes.edgecolor": INK,
    "axes.labelcolor": INK,
    "xtick.color": INK,
    "ytick.color": INK,
})

# TODO: instantiate Params, build figure with plt.subplots(...),
# call plot functions, fig.tight_layout(), plt.show()
```

## Exercises

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise 1" appearance="simple"}
<TODO: Mechanical. Plug numbers into the result of the module. The student
should be able to do this immediately after reading the Proposition.>
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Solution" collapse="true"}
<TODO: Show the arithmetic. Reference @eq-demand or other labelled results
so the solution links back to the result it applies.>
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise 2" appearance="simple"}
<TODO: Conceptual or comparative. Often the right exercise to pair with the
Warning callout — force the student to confront the misgeneralisation
explicitly by working an example where the warned-against property fails.>
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Solution" collapse="true"}
<TODO>
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Exercise 3" appearance="simple"}
<TODO: Extension. Push into duality, indirect utility, expenditure functions,
or comparative-statics objects (Slutsky decomposition, elasticities, offer
curves) that build on the headline result rather than re-using it.>
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Solution" collapse="true"}
<TODO>
:::

## References

- <TODO: MWG section, Varian chapter, JR section, etc. Keep to 2–4 sources.>
